coredata.dc:title: Colourful fluorescence-based carbon dots for tumour imaging-guided nanosurgery
coredata.dc:creator: Shang, Wenting
Xia, Xueer
Lu, Ningning
Gao, Pengli
Peng, Li
Liu, Yu
Deng, Han
Jiang, Jingying
Li, Zhou
Liu, Jianhua
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent-intraoperative navigation is a visual technique that allows surgeons to accurately distinguish malignant and normal tissues during surgery. It has the advantages of immediacy, high resolution, and high specificity. However, a single fluorescent source cannot provide sufficient surgical information. Multicolour carbon dots (CDs) are more suitable since they provide outstanding water solubility, photostability, and multicolour-fluorescence imaging. Here, we prepared an optical probe with CD-based multicolour-fluorescence imaging via a hydrothermal method. CDs can be endocytosed by tumour cells, and after intravenous injection, they can effectively accumulate at the tumour site. In a pancreatic cancer mouse model, we demonstrated the multicolour-fluorescence imaging capabilities of CDs, which aided the accurate resection of tumours under fluorescent-intraoperative navigation. Stereoscopic fluorescence microscopy imaging and H&E staining proved that the removed tissue belonged to the pancreatic tumour. This study emphasizes the potential of CDs for fluorescence-guided intraoperative resection and expands the application of CDs in biological fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Multicolour
cancer therapy
Nano-theranostic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and the biological stability of the CDs
Cellular uptake and cell imaging ability of the CDs
Fluorescence imaging-guided nanosurgery of pancreatic cancer

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Natural Science Foundation of Beijing City

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This paper is supported by grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 81971680 , 81971662 , 62027901 , 81227901 , 81930053 ), Natural Science Foundation of Beijing City (Grant No. 7202105 ) and the Research Start-up Funds for National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 8200110771 , 8190110562 ).

This paper is supported by grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 81971680, 81971662, 62027901, 81227901, 81930053), Natural Science Foundation of Beijing City (Grant No. 7202105) and the Research Start-up Funds for National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 8200110771, 8190110562). All experimental protocols were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Ethics Committee of The First Affiliation Hospital of China Medical University, and all methods were performed in accordance with the approved guidelines. This study was performed in accordance with the declaration of Helsinki. The authors confirm that the details of any images, videos, recordings, etc. in the manuscript can be published. The original contributions presented in the study are included in the article and supplementary material. Further inquiries can be directed to the corresponding author.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs. (A) TEM image of as made CDs, (B) Size distribution and (C) DLS size distribution of CDs in water. (D) HR-TEM image of as made CDs, and (E) Fourier diffraction infrared spectrum of as made CDs. (F) XRD pattern and (G) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. The high-resolution XPS of (H) C (1 s), (I) N (1 s), and (J) O (1 s).

Fluorescence spectra of CDs (A) under different excitations (from 320 to 580 nm) and (B) at different concentrations (2–5 mg·mL−1). (C) UV–vis absorption of CDs. (D) Changes of FL intensity of CDs at 525 nm vs irradiation time in FBS, water, and PBS.

(A) CLSM images of Panc-1 and MCF-7 cells incubated with different concentrations of CDs for 2 h at 37 °C. Cell images are shown in the blue channel (405 nm), the green channel (488 nm), the red channel (561 nm), in the bright field, and overlaid. Flow endocytosis of (B) CDs and CQDs in Panc-1 cells and (C) CDs and CQDs in MCF-7 cells at the different incubation times. (D) and (E) show endocytosis quantitative analysis diagrams of (B) and (C), respectively. Cell viabilities of (F) Panc-1 and (G) MCF-7 cells after incubation with CDs for 24 h by MTT assay.

(A) Biodistribution of CDs in mouse models of orthotopic breast tumour (MCF-7) and subcutaneous pancreatic tumour (Panc-1) at 560 nm. (B) Quantitative analysis of MCF-7 and Panc-1, respectively. (C) Blood vessel images of CDs before and after injection. (D) Penetration depth experiments of CDs in different thicknesses of different tissues (skin, heart, kidney, pancreas, brain, lung, liver, spleen and pancreatic tumour). (E) Quantitative analysis of (D) and Fig. S6(a-i).

(A) Orthotopic pancreatic tumour mice were monitored by intraoperative FL imaging (488 and 560 nm). The tissue showing green and red FL signals is the pancreatic tumour. Yellow arrow conveys that both 488 nm and 560 nm can be imaged at this superficial fluorescence, but the energy at 560 nm is weaker. (BC) Stereoscopic FL microscope imaging and semi-quantitative analysis of a slice of pancreatic tumour. (D) H&E slice of the orthotopic pancreatic tumour; the black box marks the tumour and the red box marks the tumour border location. (E-F) Magnified area of tumour and tumour border.

head.ce:title: Colourful fluorescence-based carbon dots for tumour imaging-guided nanosurgery

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.
These authors should be considered co-corresponding authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent-intraoperative navigation is a visual technique that allows surgeons to accurately distinguish malignant and normal tissues during surgery. It has the advantages of immediacy, high resolution, and high specificity. However, a single fluorescent source cannot provide sufficient surgical information. Multicolour carbon dots (CDs) are more suitable since they provide outstanding water solubility, photostability, and multicolour-fluorescence imaging. Here, we prepared an optical probe with CD-based multicolour-fluorescence imaging via a hydrothermal method. CDs can be endocytosed by tumour cells, and after intravenous injection, they can effectively accumulate at the tumour site. In a pancreatic cancer mouse model, we demonstrated the multicolour-fluorescence imaging capabilities of CDs, which aided the accurate resection of tumours under fluorescent-intraoperative navigation. Stereoscopic fluorescence microscopy imaging and H&E staining proved that the removed tissue belonged to the pancreatic tumour. This study emphasizes the potential of CDs for fluorescence-guided intraoperative resection and expands the application of CDs in biological fields.

an optical probe with CD-based multicolour fluorescence imaging was successfully prepared, via a hydrothermal method. In orthotopic pancreatic cancer mouse model, the probe can efficiently accumulate at the tumour site through intravenous injection. At different wavelengths, the tumour margin was accurately identified and the tumour infiltration depth was shown.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: Pancreatic cancer is a common malignancy in the digestive system and it ranks as the 7th highest cause of cancer mortality globally. 1 Even with multimodality treatments (chemotherapy, immunotherapy, and targeted therapy), surgical resection is still the only effective curative treatment. An analysis by Demir IE et al. revealed that R0 resection could significantly improve the survival rate of pancreatic cancer. 2 However, when a surgeon conducts intraoperative palpation and visual inspection, these processes are not sufficiently accurate to distinguish between malignant and normal tissue. This may result in incomplete resection or the unnecessary resection of healthy tissue. Compared with preoperative imaging examinations, intraoperative visual examinations, and palpations, in vivo fluorescence (FL) imaging has better application prospects as a precise intraoperative navigation technique, as it is real-time and has high resolution and specificity. 3–6 With the development of fluorescent intraoperative navigation technology, surgeons can more accurately distinguish tumour boundaries, identify residual tumour lesions, and completely resect lesions. 7,8

To date, many optical probes have been developed for fluorescent intraoperative navigation. However, these probes include organic small-molecule dyes with chemical postmodification, 9 semiconductor quantum dots, 10 and upconversion nanomaterials, 11,12 which can lead to some defects in the application process. For example, Tian et al. synthesized a fluorescent probe (ICG/MSNs-RGD) for the identification of liver cancer microfoci, 13 and Wang et al. designed a near-infrared (NIR-II) nanochain probe (APP-Ag2S-RGD) for detecting tumour borders 10 ; both used NIR imaging to guide surgery. Furthermore, Lei et al. designed a γ-glutamyl hydroxymethyl rhodamine green probe for removing tiny breast tumours (<1 nm) that uses fluorescent imaging to guide surgery. 14 However, these aforementioned imaging probes only exhibit monochromatic FL signals, the signal outputs of which rely on the intensity change at one wavelength. It is difficult for a monochromatic optical signal to simultaneously provide the required pathological information, such as the tumour depth and the tumour boundary information. This can result in insufficient tumour resection. Therefore, to address these issues, it is crucial to synthesize a fluorescent nanoprobe with dual or multiple emissions that are excited by a single wavelength.

Carbon dots (CDs) were first discovered by accident in 2004. 15 They constitute an attractive material that has garnered significant attention from researchers in fields such as analysis, sensors, and imaging. 16–21 CDs are widely used in biological fields due to their excellent photoluminescence properties, high FL quantum yields, biocompatibility, resistance to photobleaching, and small size; their surfaces are also easy to modify postconstruction. 22–27 Most importantly, CDs have broad spectrum properties and are considered to be ideal probes for multicolour FL imaging. Many studies have shown early-stage fluorescent CDs, but they have mainly studied single luminescence signals. 28,29 A few studies have investigated the multicolour luminescence characteristics of CDs, and they have utilized the multicolour luminescence properties of CDs in various biological fields.

However, as research has progressed, increasing attention is now being paid to the biosafety of CDs. Therefore, efforts are underway to construct CDs using synthetic raw materials constituting nutrients that are required by the human body, such as various amino acids and carbohydrates. 23,30–32 These precursors have good safety, meaning that the prepared CDs also retain good biosafety. Many studies have detailed the preparation of CDs using amino acids or carbohydrates and explored the fluorescence and physical properties of the prepared carbon dots. 32,33 For example, Xie et al. investigated the effect of different ratios of glucose and amino acids on the fluorescent and physical properties of carbon dots. 34 Currently, these CDs have been applied to various roles in biological fields.

Herein, we used glucose and amino acids (aspartic acid), essential nutrients for the human body, to prepare green-source CDs through a one-pot green synthesis method. With the help of confocal laser imaging microscopy (CLSM) and fluorescent intraoperative navigation, we utilized the multicolour fluorescent imaging properties of CDs to image the internalization of CDs by different cancer cell lines in real time. To take advantage of this property, we conducted multicolour imaging of tumours in orthotopic pancreatic cancer model mice with the aid of fluorescent intraoperative navigation equipment to accurately achieve pancreatic tumour resection. This study constitutes the first application of CDs in fluorescent intraoperative navigation to resect a tumour accurately; our findings will broaden the horizon for the application of CDs in biomedicine.

D(+)-glucose, L-aspartic acid, NaOH, and 3-[4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl]-2,5- diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Beijing Innochem Science & Technology Co., Ltd. Ultrapure water was prepared from a Milli-Q system (Millipore, USA). All other chemicals were of analytical grade or above.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images of the prepared CDs were recorded on a field emission transmission electron microscope (JEM-2100F) operating at 200 KV. The crystalline phase was investigated with an X-ray diffractometer (XRD) (D8 focus, Bruker) in the range of 5°-90° with a scan rate of 4° min-1. The sample fluorescence spectra were obtained by a spectrometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan) in a four-walled and all-transparent quartz cell with a 1 cm path length, and the excitation wavelength ranged from 320 to 580 nm. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded on a spectrophotometer (UV-3600 Plus, Shimadzu) with absorption wavelengths varying from 400 to 750 nm. Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) of CDs were analysed by using Malvern Nano-ZS90 UK. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on an IR spectrophotometer (VERTEX 70, Bruker) with the KBr pellet technique varying from 400 to 4000 cm-1. A UV lamp with a 365 nm excitation wavelength was used to continuously irradiate the aqueous sample to test the optical stability. The fluorescence surgical navigation equipment was built by our research group. Probe-based confocal laser endoscopy (Cellvizio, Mauna Kea Technologies, France) was used to qualitatively analyse the tumour vessels.

The carbon clusters were synthesized by adding D(+)-glucose (2 mmol) and L-aspartic acid (10 mmol) into a beaker containing 12 mL of aqueous NaOH (1 M). The mixture was thoroughly mixed via sonication before being moved to a Teflon-lined autoclave, heated to 125 °C for 30 min, and then heated to 200 °C for 20 min. After the crude products were diluted by adding deionized water, they were dialyzed in water using a dialysis bag (cut-off Mn: 3500) to remove unreacted molecules. Finally, the product was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The final powder of CDs was collected after freeze-drying.
The prepared CQDs had the same conditions as the CDs. The raw material was citric acid.

Human pancreatic cancer cells (Panc-1), human breast cancer cells (MCF-7) and human pancreatic ductal epithelium cells (hTERT-HPNE) were cultured in DMEM supplemented with 1 % antibiotic-antimycotic and 10 % foetal bovine serum (FBS) at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of CDs against the Panc-1-cell line and MCF-7-cell line was estimated with a methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. Cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cells per well and grown for 24 h. Then, the adherent cells were washed twice using phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution, followed by the addition of 200 μL fresh medium containing different concentrations of CDs (0 μg/mL, 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 20 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, 100 μg/mL, 200 μg/mL) for 24 h. Then, 20 μL of MTT (5 mg/mL) solution was added to each well and it was incubated for 4 h under the same conditions. Subsequently, the medium containing different concentrations of CDs was removed, 200 μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals, and the 96-well plate was shaken for 3 min. The measurement of optical density (OD) was carried out on a microplate reader (Bio-Rad I Mark Microplate reader, USA) at 490 nm. Moreover, the in vitro cytotoxicity of CDs against the hTERT-HPNE cell line was estimated with Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8) assay (in supplementary materials).

Panc-1 and MCF-7 cells were cultivated in 6-well plates at a density of 1 × 105 cells in each well and incubated for 24 h. The cells were incubated with 200 μg/mL CDs for 4 h. After that, PBS solution was used to wash the cells three times to remove the CD solution. Then, 1 mL 4 % polyoxymethylene was added and incubated for 0.5 h to fix the cells at room temperature, and the cells were washed three times with PBS solution. The uptake of the CDs by the cells was evaluated by using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). Cells that were treated with CDs were excited at 405, 488 and 561 nm.

BALB/c nude male mice (6 weeks old) were provided by Beijing Vital River Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd. (China). All experimental protocols were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Ethics Committee of The First Affiliation Hospital of China Medical University, and all methods were performed following the approved guidelines. For in vivo imaging, BALB/c nude mice were used to establish orthotopic and subcutaneous models. Briefly, Panc-1 cells and MCF-7-cell pellets were harvested by centrifugation and suspended in Matrigel matrix at a 1:1 (Vcell pellet: VMatrigel matrix) ratio for injection into breast and pancreas tissue, respectively. The animal models were allowed to grow for approximately 2 weeks. The metabolic distribution of the mice and imaging were observed with an in vivo imaging system (IVIS). With the help of fluorescent surgical navigation equipment, the orthotopic pancreatic tumour tissue was acutely resected.

Probe-based confocal laser endoscopy (Cellvizio, Mauna Kea Technologies, France) was used to qualitatively analyse the tumour vessels and the distribution of CDs in the tumour area and normal vessels. CDs (0.5 mg/mL, 200 μL) were injected into orthotopic pancreas tumour mice via the tail vein. Four hours later (when the CDs were most enriched at the tumour site), Evans Blue (excitation of 660 nm) was injected into the mice via the tail vein for imaging the blood vessels in the tumour. After 30 min, the mice were anaesthetized and placed in the prone position, and for the pancreatic tumours, a laser fibre was attached to the tumour to observe the distribution of the CDs.

CDs were prepared from D(+)-glucose and l-aspartic acid with a molar ratio of 1:5 via a hydrothermal method. 32 Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) were employed to analyse the morphologies, particle sizes and lattice structures of the CDs. As shown in Fig. 1A, the prepared CDs possessed a uniform diameter of ~3.35 ± 0.62 nm (Fig. 1B) and were monodispersed in aqueous solution. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) of the CDs was ~22 ± 5 nm (Fig. 1C). Fig. 1D shows that the prepared CDs had good lattice structures exhibiting distinct lattice fringes with a d-spacing value of 0.21 nm, corresponding to (100) graphite crystals. Fourier diffraction data (Fig. 1E) showed that the CD crystals had neat and regular geometric shapes. The X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns (Fig. 1F) of the CDs exhibited one typical peak at 21°, corresponding to the (100) plane of graphene. 16 This was consistent with the HR-TEM results, proving that the prepared CDs possessed a highly crystalline graphitic structure.

We used Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) to study the compositions of the functional surface groups of the CDs and to reveal their structural composition. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1G) revealed that the surfaces of the CDs had many functional groups containing -C=O (1625 cm−1), -C-H (2800–2700 cm−1), CC (2400 cm−1) and -N–H, -O–H (3451 cm−1). The full-scan XPS spectrum of CDs (Fig. S1) revealed that they were composed of C 1 s (283.4 eV), N 1 s (400.7 eV), O 1 s (532.7 eV) and H. The high-resolution spectrum (Fig. 1H) of C 1 s contained three peaks, C–C/C=C (284.5 eV), C–N/C–O (285.8 eV), and C=O/C=N (288.4 eV), respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 1I) contained two peaks at 399.8 eV (CN) and 401.0 eV (NH). The O 1 s band (Fig. 1J) exhibited two peaks at 531.2 eV (CO) and 533.0 eV (C=O). The XPS results (Fig. S1) were consistent with those of FT-IR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and the biological stability of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The optical properties of the prepared CDs were evaluated by using photoluminescence (PL) spectra and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption. The emission spectra (Fig. 2A) demonstrated that the CDs exhibited significant excitation-dependent behaviour. The optimal emission peak of the CDs occurred at 525 nm under excitation at 400 nm. Under different excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 580 nm, the emission range of CDs was 400–700 nm. Therefore, the prepared CDs exhibited multicolour fluorescent imaging, laying the foundation for subsequent multicolour imaging either in vitro or in vivo.

The prepared CDs also demonstrated concentration-dependent fluorescent characteristics (Fig. 2B). When the concentration was >50 μg·mL−1, the FL intensity of the CDs gradually decreased, which may have been caused by aggregation induction. The CDs also exhibited concentration-dependent FL emission, which is one of the most commonly observed phenomena of CDs.

The UV–Vis absorption spectrum revealed that the absorption wavelength varied from 400 to 750 nm (Fig. 2C), showing that the CDs had good absorption in the range of 400–750 nm. The photostability of the CDs was evaluated by PL spectra. Moreover, to detect the FL stability in vivo, the CDs were tested in a variety of solvents. As shown in Fig. 2D, the PL intensity of the CDs decreased slightly in foetal bovine serum (FBS) after light irradiation for 240 h, indicating that they had excellent photostability in FBS. TEM imaging showed the integrity of the CDs after 240 h in FBS (Fig. 2D). The FL intensity of the CDs gradually decreased with increasing irradiation time, both in water and in PBS. The above results prove that the prepared CDs were capable of multicolour imaging and, thus, could be suitably applied to biological imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and cell imaging ability of the CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Studying the optical properties of the prepared CDs revealed their multicolour imaging ability. Therefore, we performed experiments at the cellular level for further verification. The in vitro fluorescent imaging capabilities of the CDs were investigated using CLSM. As shown in Fig. 3A, Panc-1 and MCF-7 cells were incubated at a CD concentration of 200 μg·mL−1 for 2 h at 37 °C. Under excitation at 405, 488, and 561 nm, the Panc-1 and MCF-7 cells emitted blue, green, and red FL, respectively. To prove their universality, liver cancer cells (HepG2) were also validated (Fig. S2). These results prove that the prepared CDs exhibited multicolour imaging capability.

In addition, with the help of a flow cytometer, we further compared the endocytosis of Panc-1 and MCF-7 cells that were treated with different concentrations (0–200 μg·mL−1) of CQDs and CDs. Fig. 3B and C show that the CDs and CQDs both exhibited concentration-dependent endocytosis; the effect was far stronger for CDs than for CQDs. FL quantification (Fig. 3D and E) also showed that the CDs were more easily endocytosed by cells than CQDs. The above cell experiments demonstrated that the CDs were easily endocytosed by cancer cells, demonstrating the advantages of CDs for multicolour imaging of cells. At the same time, the multicolour imaging of cells is important for using fluorescent intraoperative navigation to resect tumours accurately.

Moreover, biological safety is a key factor for the bioapplication of CDs. The cytotoxicity of the CDs was investigated in Panc-1 and MCF-7 using an MTT assay and hTERT-HPNE cells using a CCK8 assay. As shown in Fig. 3F, the survival rates of Panc-1 cells were all >85 % after incubation with different concentrations of CDs for 24 h, indicating that the CDs did not cause cell death. MCF-7 cells and hTERT-HPNE cells were incubated with the same concentration of CDs as Panc-1 cells (Fig. 3G and Fig. S3), achieving an MCF-7 cell viability of >90 % and an hTERT-HPNE cell viability of >97 %. The above results show that the CDs have outstanding biocompatibility.

The MTT and CLSM experiments proved that the CDs displayed excellent biocompatibility and could be successfully endocytosed by Panc-1 and MCF-7 cells. To identify the imaging distribution of CDs in vivo, we constructed both orthotopic breast tumour and subcutaneous pancreatic tumour models. We employed CDs (0.5 mg·mL−1, 200 μL) as probes and CQDs (0.5 mg·mL−1, 200 μL) as control probes to visually image the animal models, which were intravenously injected into the mice through their tails. A high-resolution animal live imager was used for in vivo imaging at different time points (pre, 0.5, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, and 24 h). As shown in Fig. 4A and Fig. S4, the CDs showed a better ability to accumulate in the tumour in both models; the accumulations reached their maximums at 12 h. In addition, it is also clear from our dissection of the Panc-1 model injected with CDs that the probes have a well-targeted accumulation effect within the tumour tissue (Fig. S5). The above results prove that the prepared CDs demonstrated targeted-tumour efficiency. The high accumulation of CDs in tumour cells could be attributed to their enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect. The tumour-to-background ratio (TBR) revealed that the best survey time windows were 2–8 and 4–24 h after intravenous injection for the subcutaneous tumour (breast cancer) and deep tumour (pancreatic cancer), respectively (Fig. 4B).

With the help of the Cellvizio system (Mauna Kea Technologies, Paris, France), we further investigated the targeted-tumour efficiencies of CDs in the MCF-7 breast tumour and Panc-1 subcutaneous pancreatic tumour mouse models. As the prepared CDs exhibited multicolour imaging (blue, green, and red FL), after intravenously injecting the CDs, we observed that green FL could be monitored in the 488 nm channel (Fig. 4C). We used Evans Blue to stain the blood vessels in the tumour, revealing that red FL could be monitored in the 660 nm channel. The merged image proves that the CDs were suitably enriched in the tumour blood vessels. However, before the CDs were injected, no green FL was observed in the 488 nm channel. The Cellvizio images revealed that the CDs can target tumour vessels to achieve cumulative effects at the tumour site.

We then studied the penetrating ability of the CDs. 35 We placed chicken breast slices of the same thickness on a 96-well plate, with the thickness of each hole increasing in turn (the last hole served as a control group). As shown in Fig. 4D (a-i), these samples corresponded to skin, heart, kidney, pancreas, brain, lung, liver, spleen, and tumour tissues. A high-resolution in vivo imager was used to study the penetration depths of the CDs into different-thickness slices of tissues and organs. The imaging results (Fig. 4D (a-i) and Fig. S6) revealed the penetration depths of the CDs for skin, heart, kidney, pancreas, brain, lung, liver, spleen, and tumour tissues to be 400 μm (four layers), 300 μm (three layers), 400 μm (four layers), 500 μm (five layers), 600 μm (six layers), 500 μm (five layers), 500 μm (five layers), 500 μm (five layers), and 1000 μm (ten layers), respectively. The corresponding imaging signal-to-noise ratios were 1.4, 7.2, 28.1, 36.6, 39.2, 27.3, 27.2, 15.0, and 31.8, respectively. Analysis of the signal-to-noise ratio (Fig. 4E) revealed that the CDs had a high signal-to-noise ratio in the pancreas and achieved their deepest penetration depth into the tumour. Therefore, the above experimental results prove that the CDs have better imaging effects in pancreatic tumours.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence imaging-guided nanosurgery of pancreatic cancer
ce:section.ce:para: In clinical oncology, intraoperative imaging has great guiding significance for the accurate resection of a tumour, thereby improving the effects of surgery. Multicolour FL imaging provides a better imaging method for accurately distinguishing a tumour's boundary. Here, we have shown that CDs exhibit multicolour FL imaging. Encouraged by the in vivo imaging and penetration depth experiments, we constructed an orthotopic pancreatic cancer model in BALB/c nude mice. The multicolour imaging properties of the CDs were used to verify whether pancreatic tumours could be accurately resected under the guidance of FL imaging.

As shown in Fig. 5A, 12 h after injection of the CDs they had reached their maximum accumulation at the tumour site. The tumour was accurately imaged at both 488 and 560 nm by fluorescent surgical navigation. This imaging capability will improve a surgeon's ability to precisely resect a tumour. Under the guidance of multicolour FL imaging, surgical resection was performed, and we observed that the tumour was completely removed.

We performed FL imaging of both the resected tumour and the mice, which confirmed that the resected tissue was tumorous. Then, with the help of a stereo FL microscope, the tumour slices of Fig. 5Aiii were imaged (Fig. 5B and Fig. S7). They demonstrated good FL imaging effects at wavelengths of 488 and 560 nm, and stereo imaging also proved that the CDs effectively accumulated in the tumour. Fig. 5B (c and e) clearly shows the tumour tissue under FL at 488 nm, with the tumour margin being easily identifiable (Fig. 5B(c) red arrow). Furthermore, FL at 560 nm clearly revealed the tumour infiltration depth (Fig. 5B (e) yellow arrow). The FL image highlighted by the arrow in Fig. 5B (e) shows that at 560 nm, more tumour cells infiltrated the deep tissue. This image aided the removal of more tissue, allowing for the tumour to be completely resected (Fig. 5A(iv)).

Quantitative analysis revealed that the 488 nm band could be used for margin discovery, whereas the 560 nm band could more easily provide more information regarding the tumour infiltration depth (Fig. 5C). Furthermore, histological analysis of haematoxylin and eosin (H&E) stained tissues confirmed that the resected tissue was tumorous (Fig. 5D-F). We intercepted two positions in Fig. 5D for magnification analysis (black frame for Fig. 5E and red frame for Fig. 5F). Fig. 5E and F show information on the deep tissue and the margin, respectively. These results show that CD-based multicolour FL imaging can accurately identify the margins of a tumour and its deep tissue, thereby improving the efficacy of tumour surgery and greatly reducing the risk of in situ tumour recurrence.

Multispectral imaging has superior properties, such as being noninvasive, 36 removing spectral overlap interference 37 and distinguishing between surgical targets and surrounding vital structures. 38 Therefore, the construction of multispectral probes can provide more information for tumour surgical resection. Carbon is an important material for the human body, and the construction of carbon quantum dots from natural materials can improve the biosafety of carbon materials. As glucose and amino acids are important components of the human body, CDs synthesized from them can greatly improve the safety of the material. Hence, CDs with the multicolour fluorescent imaging function were successfully prepared via a hydrothermal reaction using glucose and amino acids as starting materials.

To show that the CDs were well prepared, we analysed their morphologies, particle sizes and lattice structures using TEM and HR-TEM, as shown in Fig. 1A-E. The evidence in Figs. 1 and 2 suggests that the prepared CDs exhibited excellent water solubility and good stability. This proves that the prepared CDs possess a highly crystalline graphitic structure and good stability for further in vivo detection.

Multicolour is an important property of the CDs. Therefore, it is very important to verify their multicolour properties. Under different excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 580 nm, we found that the emission range of CDs was 400–700 nm, as shown in Fig. 2A, which indicated that the prepared CDs had the ability of multicolour imaging. The imaging range of the CDs was from visible wavelengths to near infrared wavelengths.

The safety and tumour affinity of this probe are very important for further in vivo detection. Therefore, the cytotoxicity was tested with cell viability assays as shown in Fig. 3F-G, indicating that the prepared CDs have outstanding biocompatibility, providing strong support for subsequent in vivo experiments. Furthermore, drawing on an extensive range of sources, Yuan et al. explored the effect of surface functional groups on the properties of carbon dots. 39 We prepared the CDs using glucose and amino acids as starting materials, which were energy-providing substances. Therefore, the residues of these substances were retained in the carbon dots during the synthesis process. Moreover, there are a large number of published studies that describe tumours as fast-growing organisms that need a large supply of energy materials, such as glucose. 40–42 When a carbon dot enters the body, it will be recognized by the tumour as an energy substance and extensively absorbed. Compared with other traditional CQDs, this probe has certain tumour adsorption and targeting properties.

Then, we verified the targeting of CDs in animal experiments. With the help of a high-resolution animal real-time imager and the Cellvizio system, we proved that the prepared CDs could effectively accumulate in the tumour site and adsorb around the vascular walls of the tumour. Through penetration depth experiments of CDs in different tissues, we confirmed that they exhibited a high signal-to-noise ratio in pancreatic tumours.

After surgical resection of a nude mouse model of orthotopic pancreatic cancer, we further explored the resected tumour tissues by the multicolour property of our probe. Fig. 5B (c and e) reveals that the 488 nm band provides a good outline of the tumour margin and that the 560 nm band is expressed better at the tumour infiltration depth. Through quantitative analysis (Fig. 5C), we demonstrated that the 488 nm band could provide more data on tumour margins, while on the contrary, the 560 nm band could provide more data on tumour depth. Combined with the above research, it was demonstrated that an in situ pancreatic tumour could be accurately resected with the help of the fluorescent intraoperative navigation and multicolour imaging functions of the CDs.

Generally, this study is the first to report the use of CDs for intrafluoroscopy navigation. The findings presented here demonstrate that CDs can be used as a multicolour optical probe for fluorescent intraoperative navigation with excellent visualization of tumour margins and depth, which can help to accurately identify and resect tumour tissue. This study highlights that CDs have great potential for clinical applications.

Wenting Shang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Xueer Xia: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Ningning Lu: Investigation, Data curation, Validation. Pengli Gao: Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis. Li Peng: Investigation, Data curation. Yu Liu: Investigation, Visualization. Han Deng: Resources, Investigation, Visualization. Jingying Jiang: Conceptualization, Resources, Funding acquisition. Zhou Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Jianhua Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para: This paper is supported by grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 81971680, 81971662, 62027901, 81227901, 81930053), Natural Science Foundation of Beijing City (Grant No. 7202105) and the Research Start-up Funds for National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 8200110771, 8190110562).

All experimental protocols were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Ethics Committee of The First Affiliation Hospital of China Medical University, and all methods were performed in accordance with the approved guidelines. This study was performed in accordance with the declaration of Helsinki.
The authors confirm that the details of any images, videos, recordings, etc. in the manuscript can be published.
The original contributions presented in the study are included in the article and supplementary material. Further inquiries can be directed to the corresponding author.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.nano.2022.102583.
